name: Swift
description: Apple's modern language for iOS, macOS, and beyond. Safe, fast, and expressive with a clean syntax.

lessons.title: Hello World & print()
lessons.content: Swift is Apple's language for iOS and macOS apps. The print() function outputs to the console. Swift is type-safe and uses type inference — you rarely need to write types explicitly.
lessons.code_example:
print("Hello, World!")
print(42)

let name = "Alice"
print("Hello, \(name)!")  // string interpolation
print(3 + 4)               // 7

exercises.question: What syntax is used for string interpolation in Swift?

lessons.title: let, var & Optionals
lessons.content: Swift uses 'let' for constants and 'var' for variables. Optionals (Type?) represent a value that may be absent. Use if-let or guard-let to safely unwrap optionals.
lessons.code_example:
let pi = 3.14159          // constant
var score = 0             // variable
score += 10

var city: String? = nil   // optional
city = "Casablanca"

if let c = city {
    print("City: \(c)")   // City: Casablanca
}

exercises.question: What does String? mean in Swift?
options: An optional String that may be nil
exercises.answer: An optional String that may be nil
hints: The ? marks it as optional — it can hold nil

exercises.question: Which pattern safely unwraps an optional in Swift?
hints: if let value = optional { } — safe unwrapping

lessons.title: Structs & Classes
lessons.content: Swift has both structs (value types) and classes (reference types). Structs are copied when assigned; classes share a reference. In Swift, prefer structs for simple data models.

exercises.question: What is the key difference between structs and classes in Swift?
options: Structs support inheritance, classes do not
Structs are value types (copied); classes are reference types (shared)
exercises.answer: Structs are value types (copied); classes are reference types (shared)
hints: Copy vs reference — structs are independent copies

lessons.title: Closures
lessons.content: Closures are self-contained blocks of code that can be passed and used. They are Swift's equivalent of lambdas. Trailing closure syntax makes them very clean. Used heavily in SwiftUI and async code.

exercises.question: What do $0 and $1 refer to inside a Swift closure?
options: Shorthand names for the first and second arguments
exercises.answer: Shorthand names for the first and second arguments
hints: Swift automatically provides $0, $1... as argument shorthand
